answer:
In the Urdu poetry corpus—primarily *Bang-E-Dara* (Parts 1–3) by Allama Iqbal, *Diwan-e-Ghalib* by Mirza Ghalib, and *Diwan-e-Zauq* by Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq—'maut' (موت, death) is a pervasive motif, appearing dozens of times across ghazals and nazms.[^1] It is rarely treated as abstract philosophy but evoked emotionally and metaphorically: as inevitable fate, a piercing emotional torment, a transition to mystery, or even a longed-for release in love or martyrdom. Poets use it to underscore life's transience (*fana*), the soul's restlessness (*betaabi*), and contrasts between worldly illusion (*dunya*) and eternal truth (*haq*).

### Philosophical Inquiry and Mystery (Iqbal)
Iqbal most directly philosophizes 'maut', portraying it as an enigma bridging this world and the next. In "Mehr-e-Roshan" (*Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 32), the speaker addresses the dead ("sleepers in dust") with rapid-fire questions: Does the afterlife mirror earthly sorrows (nightingale's lament, moth in flame, kinships as thorns)? Is Paradise Beauty unveiled or mere rest? Hell a purifying fire? Is death (*maut*) "a piercing thorn in human hearts," concealing the "turning dome" (*gumbad-e-gardaan*)'s secret?[^2] Here, death is not finality but a "long night" prompting cosmic thirst (*iztiraab-e-dil*), akin to Sinai's divine refusal ("Lan Tarani").[^3]

Iqbal elsewhere links it to renewal: martyrdom (*maut ke zehraab*) yields life (*zindagi*), as in a youth's joyful death en route to Medina (*Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 6).[^4] Or true life defies death's decree (*Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 42).[^5]

### Inevitability and Longing (Ghalib)
Ghalib weaves 'maut' into existential despair and ironic longing. In his iconic ghazal "Koi ummeed bar nahin aati" (*Diwan-e-Ghalib*, ch. 115), death's appointed day (*muayyan*) causes sleeplessness amid unfulfilled hopes; one dies *wanting* to die (*marte hain aarzoo mein marne ki*), yet it eludes.[^6] Life's prison (*qaid-e-hayaat*) and grief's chain (*band-e-gham*) are "one" (*asl mein donon ek hain*), freeing only at death—though even then, shy of the Kaaba.[^7] It personifies as *malik-ul-maut* (lord of death, ch. 125) or something summonable like a lover (ch. 135).[^8][^9]

### Remembrance and Romance (Zauq)
Zauq uses 'maut' didactically or romantically: "Maut insaan ko laazim hai sadaa yaad rahe" (death is man's constant reminder, ch. 11).[^10] It battles healing (*shifa se ladti hai*, ch. 1), lovers crave it like Laila (*maze jo maut ke aashiq*, ch. 36), or strikes via beloved's glance (*zindagi-o-maut apni*, ch. 42).[^11][^12][^13] Life signals impending death (*zindagi maut ke aane ki khabar deti hai*, ch. 51).[^14]

Across the corpus, 'maut' evokes *fana* (annihilation in love/divine) over fear, aligning with Sufi undertones: a thorn, door, or wish, not mere end. No systematic treatise exists—these are poetic flashes.

[^1]: Aggregated from 24+ transliteration matches via *search_corpus* ("maut"); English translations use "death."
[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 32 ("Mehr-e-Roshan"), translation, [full chapter](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/32-mehr-e-roshan/translation/).
[^3]: Ibid., [p-d665a1](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/32-mehr-e-roshan/translation/#p-d665a1).
[^4]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 6 ("Ek haji Madinay ke raastay mein"), transliteration, [p-51cb6d](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/06-ek-haji-madinay-ke-raastay-mein/transliteration/#p-51cb6d).
[^5]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 42 ("Subah Ka Sitara"), transliteration, [p-ef557a](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/42-subah-ka-sitara/transliteration/#p-ef557a).
[^6]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 115 ("Koi ummeed bar nahin aati"), transliteration, [full chapter](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/115-koi-ummeed-bar-nahin-aati/transliteration/).
[^7]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 33 ("Dil hi to hai na sang-o-khisht..."), transliteration, [full chapter](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/33-dil-hi-to-hai-na-sang-o-khisht-dard-se-bhar-na-aay/transliteration/).
[^8]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 125 ("Lazim tha ke dekho mera rasta..."), transliteration, [full chapter](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/125-lazim-tha-ke-dekho-mera-rasta-koi-din-aur/transliteration/).
[^9]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 135 ("Meherbaan ho ke bula lo..."), transliteration, [full chapter](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/135-meherbaan-ho-ke-bula-lo-mujhe-chaaho-jis-waqt/transliteration/).
[^10]: Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, *Diwan-E-Zauq*, ch. 11 ("Chashm-e-qaatil humein kyonkar na bhala yaad rahe"), transliteration, [p-900848](/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/11-chashm-e-qaatil-humein-kyonkar-na-bhala-yaad-rahe/transliteration/#p-900848).
[^11]: Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, *Diwan-E-Zauq*, ch. 1 ("Aankh us pur-jafa se ladti hai"), transliteration, [full chapter](/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/01-aankh-us-pur-jafa-se-ladti-hai/transliteration/).
[^12]: Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, *Diwan-E-Zauq*, ch. 36 ("Maza tha hum ko jo Laila se do-ba-do karte"), transliteration, [p-510293](/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/36-maza-tha-hum-ko-jo-laila-se-do-ba-do-karte/transliteration/#p-510293).
[^13]: Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, *Diwan-E-Zauq*, ch. 42 ("Neemcha yaar ne jis waqt baghal mein maara"), transliteration, [p-65978b](/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/42-neemcha-yaar-ne-jis-waqt-baghal-mein-maara/transliteration/#p-65978b).
[^14]: Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, *Diwan-E-Zauq*, ch. 51 ("Yeh iqaamat hamein paigham-e-safar deti hai"), transliteration, [p-59c1c2](/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/51-yeh-iqaamat-hamein-paigham-e-safar-deti-hai/transliteration/#p-59c1c2).
